title: The path that breaks more sites than anything else
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of fixing the path. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
why.no-href: The link has no address, or it is empty. Without href it leads nowhere.
why.local-path: The path still points at your own computer — C:\Users\… That address exists only on your machine. Upload the site and the link leads nowhere for everyone else.
hint2.local-path: Look at the href. Is there a drive letter, a username, or the word Desktop in it?
hint3.local-path: This path says "the file is at this spot on the disk". It should say "the file is over there, relative to me".
hint4.local-path: Delete everything before the filename. The two files are in one folder — so the name is enough.
why.not-relative: The path is still not relative. The two files sit next to each other — you write just the name: contact.html. Nothing more, no folders in front.
hint2.not-relative: Where is contact.html relative to the page you are on? In the same folder.
hint3.not-relative: about me — a file next to me. That is all there is to it.
hint4.not-relative: href="contact.html". Just the filename, nothing else.
why.lost-link: The link or its words are gone. The task is to fix the path, not to delete things.
blocks.0.text: You are making a second page — for contacts. You link it from the first. You copy the path out of Explorer and paste it into href.
blocks.1.text: It works! On your computer. You open it, you click, it goes. Everything is fine.
blocks.4.text: This address exists on exactly one computer in the world — yours. Tomorrow you upload the site and the link leads nowhere for everyone else.
blocks.5.text: This is the mistake that breaks more beginner sites than any other.
blocks.8.text: That is all. If the two files sit next to each other, you write only the name. The browser reads it relative to where the current page is — hence "relative".
blocks.9.text: Do not ask where the file is on disk. Ask where it is relative to me.
blocks.11.items.0: contact.html — a file next to me, in the same folder
blocks.11.items.1: pages/contact.html — a file in the pages folder, inside mine
blocks.12.text: And a fourth — the one that starts with a slash: /contact.html. It means "from the root of the site", wherever I am. Useful on a large site, but it will not work while the files sit on your own computer.
blocks.14.text: There is one question that settles everything: if I take the whole site folder, send it to another person, and they open it on their machine — will it work?
blocks.15.text: If your code contains a drive letter, a username, or the word "Desktop" — the answer is no.
blocks.16.text: A site is a folder, not a place on a disk.
blocks.17.text: One warning to finish with: on your computer paths ignore upper and lower case. On a server — they do not. Bike.jpg and bike.jpg are two different files there.
blocks.18.text: That is where the classic line comes from: "it works on my machine".
